title: Do You Love Your Hero and His Two-Hit Multi-Target Attacks?
description: Akito decided to study a new powerful spell. Since it possesses immeasurable strength, it certainly requires a lot of space and careful preparation. For this, Akito went out into the field. Let's represent the field as a Cartesian coordinate system. For the spell, Akito needs to place $$$0 \le n \le 500$$$ staffs at distinct integer coordinates in the field such that there will be exactly $$$k$$$ pairs $$$(i, j)$$$ such that $$$1 \le i  \lt  j \le n$$$ and $$$\rho(i, j) = d(i, j)$$$. Here, for two points with integer coordinates $$$a = (x_a, y_a)$$$ and $$$b = (x_b, y_b)$$$, $$$\rho(a, b) = \sqrt{(x_a - x_b)^2 + (y_a - y_b)^2}$$$ and $$$d(a, b) = |x_a - x_b| + |y_a - y_b|$$$.
input_spec: The first line of input contains a single number $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 1000$$$) — the number of test cases. In the only line of each test case, there is a single number $$$k$$$ ($$$0 \le k \le 10^5$$$) — the number of pairs of staffs for which the equality $$$\rho(i, j) = d(i, j)$$$ must hold.
output_spec: For each test case, the first line of output should print the number $$$n$$$ ($$$0 \le n \le 500$$$) — the number of placed staffs. In the following $$$n$$$ lines, pairs of integers $$$x_i, y_i$$$ $$$(-10^9 \le x_i, y_i \le 10^9)$$$ should be printed — the coordinates of the $$$i$$$-th staff. The points in which staffs stand must be distinct.

routes.name: Greedy collinear-group decomposition
routes.description: Characterize when Euclidean equals Manhattan, reduce the problem to constructing groups of axis-aligned points, then greedily decompose k into triangular numbers to determine group sizes.

observations.title: Euclidean equals Manhattan iff the points are axis-aligned
observations.description: For two points a and b, rho(a,b) = d(a,b) holds if and only if they share an x-coordinate or share a y-coordinate. Squaring both sides and expanding reveals that the equality reduces to |dx|*|dy| = 0, meaning one coordinate difference must be zero.

hints.nudge: When does the Euclidean distance between two points equal their Manhattan distance? Try small examples with specific coordinates.
hints.question: Square both sides of rho = d. Expand sqrt((dx)^2 + (dy)^2) = |dx| + |dy|. After squaring, what simplification occurs? What must be true about dx and dy?
hints.formal: rho(a,b)^2 = dx^2 + dy^2. d(a,b)^2 = (|dx| + |dy|)^2 = dx^2 + 2|dx||dy| + dy^2. Equality requires 2|dx||dy| = 0, so dx = 0 or dy = 0. The points must share an x-coordinate (vertical alignment) or a y-coordinate (horizontal alignment).

observations.title: A group of m collinear points contributes C(m,2) valid pairs
observations.description: If m points all lie on the same horizontal or vertical line, every pair among them satisfies the distance equality. The number of such pairs is m*(m-1)/2. This is the building block for constructing exactly k pairs.

hints.nudge: If you place several points on one horizontal line, how many valid pairs do they form?
hints.question: Among m points on a shared horizontal line, every pair is axis-aligned. How many pairs exist? What familiar formula gives this count?
hints.formal: m collinear points (same row or column) produce C(m,2) = m*(m-1)/2 valid pairs. Points from different lines that share neither x nor y contribute zero valid pairs.

observations.title: Groups on separate lines must not share any coordinate
observations.description: If two groups lie on different horizontal lines, points from different groups could still form valid pairs if they share an x-coordinate. To ensure only within-group pairs count, place each group on its own horizontal line with x-ranges that do not overlap with any other group's x-coordinates, and use distinct y-values across groups.

hints.nudge: If you have two horizontal groups at different y-values, could a point from one group still form a valid pair with a point from the other?
hints.question: Two points on different horizontal lines satisfy the condition only if they share an x-coordinate. How can you place groups so that no cross-group x-sharing occurs?
hints.formal: Place group i on the horizontal line y = y_i with x-coordinates in a range disjoint from all other groups. For example, use consecutive integer x-values and offset each group. This ensures cross-group pairs never share x or y, contributing exactly zero extra valid pairs.

observations.title: Decompose k as a sum of triangular numbers greedily
observations.description: Each group of size m contributes C(m,2) = m*(m-1)/2 pairs. To achieve exactly k total pairs, greedily pick the largest m such that m*(m-1)/2 <= k, subtract that from k, and repeat. This is equivalent to writing k as a sum of triangular numbers T(m-1) = C(m,2).

hints.nudge: You need exactly k pairs total. Each group contributes a triangular number of pairs. How do you split k into these chunks?
hints.question: If you greedily take the largest triangular number T(m) = m*(m+1)/2 that does not exceed k, subtract it, and repeat, will this always terminate? Will the total number of points stay within 500?
hints.formal: At each step find the largest m with m*(m-1)/2 <= k. Set k' = k - m*(m-1)/2 and place m points on a new line. Repeat until k' = 0. Since C(m,2) grows quadratically, m ~ sqrt(2k), and the total number of points across all groups is O(sqrt(k)). For k <= 10^5, the total n is well under 500.

observations.title: The total point count fits within the n <= 500 bound
observations.description: The greedy decomposition produces groups whose total size is bounded. The first group has size roughly sqrt(2k), and the remainder shrinks fast. For k = 10^5, a single group of 449 points gives C(449,2) = 100576 >= 10^5, so the total never exceeds 500 points. This confirms the construction is always valid.

hints.nudge: How many points does the greedy approach use in total? Is it always at most 500?
hints.question: The first group takes the bulk of k. After subtracting C(m,2), the leftover is at most m-1. What does this imply about the total point count across all groups?
hints.formal: After choosing group size m, the remainder r = k - C(m,2) satisfies r <= m-2. The next group has size roughly sqrt(2r). Total points sum like m + sqrt(m) + sqrt(sqrt(m)) + ..., which converges rapidly. For k <= 10^5, the maximum total n is around 450, comfortably within 500.
